quote: Okay. Gotcha. Do you know by chance when you'd be available for an appointment?
stewart_read: Carter skips the entire verify section — 0 of 8 items captured — and jumps directly to scheduling. No spouse/co-owner check, no utility company, no prior designs, no bill anchor, no roof age, no interest-in-solar depth. This is the most consequential structural gap in the call. The roofing cross-sell and the appointment both land despite this skip, but the closer walks in blind on qualification.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter already has prior-call data from Meg's interaction and is deliberately compressing verify to avoid re-interrogating a warm recycled lead. Rejected — Carter asks 'where did we leave off with you?' at 00:22, indicating he does not have Meg's notes in front of him and is reconstructing context live. The skip is not strategic compression; it's a structural miss.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1 and walk Carter through what the closer is missing: Is there a spouse on title? What's the utility company? Has Ken seen a prior design? What's the actual bill amount? Carter got lucky that Ken volunteered the roof situation — but none of the required verify items were captured. Manager should reinforce that recycled lead status does not waive verify; it may compress it, but the co-owner check and bill anchor are non-negotiable.

quote: I got a HELOC coming through. I'm planning on using that to I I need to redo my roof.
stewart_read: Ken volunteers a HELOC and a planned roof replacement unprompted. This is a high-value enthusiasm signal — the customer has financing lined up and a concrete near-term project. Carter does eventually bridge to the roofing cross-sell at 02:05, which is the right move. Credit where it's due: the cross-sell lands. However, Carter doesn't anchor the HELOC as a financing vehicle for the combined roof+solar package, which is a missed depth opportunity.
coaching_implication: Carter gets partial credit here — he bridged to roofing. The coaching moment is the HELOC: a customer who says 'I have financing coming through' is telling you the money conversation is already open. Manager should show Carter how to tie the HELOC explicitly to the bundle ('that HELOC could cover both at once — that's exactly the kind of thing our specialist can structure for you'). No fabricated numbers needed — the leverage is the customer's own financing signal.

quote: Yeah. So we don't do ground mounts. What we could do for you, though, is obviously a solar estimate as well as a roof estimate.
stewart_read: Carter correctly bridges to the roofing cross-sell and the customer responds positively. However, Carter leads with a 'no' ('we don't do ground mounts') before pivoting to the 'yes.' The codex template opens with the value proposition, not the limitation. More importantly, Carter frames this as 'a solar estimate as well as a roof estimate' — two separate things — rather than the bundled, integrated framing the codex calls for ('get the roof done right with solar planned into it from day one'). The cross-sell lands but at lower conviction than it could.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter deliberately keeps the framing simple to avoid overwhelming a customer who just said he's in planning mode. Plausible, but the customer's immediate 'Okay. Yeah. Let me see what tomorrow looks like' suggests he was ready for more — the simple framing didn't cost the booking, but it may have undersold the bundle's value.
coaching_implication: Manager should show Carter the codex template for roof_replacement_planned. The move is to lead with the integration story ('roof done right with solar planned in from day one') before mentioning the two separate estimates. The customer's HELOC signal makes this even more relevant — the bundle framing + financing vehicle is a natural pairing. Carter got the cross-sell; the coaching is on depth and sequencing.

quote: I'm not in a massive rush to get this done. I'm just in the planning stages.
stewart_read: Ken explicitly names his pace and his planning posture. Carter's response is to immediately ask about appointment availability — no acknowledgment of the timeline, no validation of Ken's approach, no energy match. The miss doesn't kill the call (Ken self-books 90 seconds later), but it's a trust-building gap. A customer who says 'I'm not in a rush' and gets a scheduling push back is being subtly told his pace doesn't matter.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter is deliberately not dwelling on the 'not in a rush' framing to avoid reinforcing it as a reason to delay. Partially defensible — dwelling on it could entrench the objection. But the codex's energy_match principle calls for matching the customer's tempo, not overriding it. A brief acknowledgment ('totally makes sense — that's exactly why we do the estimate first, no pressure to move until you're ready') would have matched pace without reinforcing delay.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to illustrate energy_match: Ken gave Carter a clear signal about his decision-making style. One sentence of validation before the scheduling ask would have built trust without slowing the call. The coaching is not 'be more empathetic' — it's 'when a customer names their pace, mirror it for one beat before moving forward.'

quote: Also have options for possibly putting panels on the ground. I don't even know if ION has those, but that's another option I wanted to look at.
stewart_read: Ken raises ground mounts as a question. Carter answers directly ('we don't do ground mounts') — this is accurate and within scope. However, Carter doesn't use the universal escape hatch to bridge to the specialist for any follow-on questions about ground mount alternatives or system design. The answer is clean but the bridge is missing. No scope creep here, but the opportunity to create curiosity about what the specialist CAN show him is left on the table.
coaching_implication: Minor coaching point. Carter's answer is correct. The refinement is to add a forward bridge: 'Our specialist can walk you through what we can do on your specific roof layout — that's exactly the kind of thing the design call covers.' Keeps scope clean and creates curiosity rather than closing a door.

quote: Yeah. And, I mean, same thing as before, in order to get you on a schedule, we would need that utility bill. Do you get those through the mail or online?
stewart_read: Carter re-raises the bill requirement after the appointment time is agreed, which is the correct sequence for a recycled lead where the prior rep (Meg) already requested the bill. However, the appointment is verbally confirmed before the bill is in hand, creating a conditional booking. If this is a paid lead, the protocol requires the bill before the appointment can be set — Carter is inverting the sequence. Additionally, Carter does not check whether Ion already has Ken's bill from Meg's prior interaction, which would make re-collection redundant.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter locks the time slot first to create commitment, then re-raises the bill as a lower-stakes ask — Ken is more likely to send the bill once he's mentally committed to the appointment. This is a defensible sequencing choice for a warm recycled lead. However, it only works if the bill actually arrives before the appointment, which is not guaranteed.
coaching_implication: Manager should flag two things: (1) Check whether Ion already has Ken's bill from Meg's interaction — if so, re-collection is wasted time and the appointment should have been confirmed clean. (2) If this is a paid lead, the bill-before-booking protocol is inverted here. Carter should be coached on the lead-type rule: paid leads require the bill before the slot is locked, not after.

quote: spot you out for an hour, and they'll give you a roof estimate as well as a solar estimate.
stewart_read: Carter's button-up is incomplete. He states the time (09:30) and the one-hour block, which are correct. Missing: no explicit phone appointment specification, no 'not driving / at your computer' anchor, no 'is that fair?' verbal handshake, and no text follow-up promise. The codex requires all five elements. The missing 'is that fair?' handshake is the highest-risk omission — it's the verbal commitment that correlates with show rate. The missing text follow-up promise is a secondary gap.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk Carter through the full button-up checklist against this call. Carter got the time and the hour block. He missed the computer/not-driving anchor, the verbal handshake, and the text promise. For a customer who is explicitly 'not in a rush' and has a conditional bill commitment outstanding, the 'is that fair?' handshake is especially important — it's the moment Ken verbally commits to showing up. Without it, the show risk on this appointment is higher than it needs to be.

quote: Alright. Appreciate it, Cutter. K. Of course. Look forward to hearing back from you.
stewart_read: Carter closes the call without confirming he will send a follow-up text. The codex requires a text follow-up promise with a tieback to something specific the customer said. Ken gave Carter multiple tieback anchors: the HELOC, the roof replacement, the spring timeline, the college football / cross country context. None of these are referenced in the close. The call ends with 'look forward to hearing back from you' — which is passive (Carter is waiting on Ken's bill) rather than active (Carter confirming next steps he will take).
coaching_implication: Manager should show Carter the text_after_call template and point to the tieback requirement. Ken gave rich material: 'Hey Ken, this is Carter with ION — I have you on the calendar for tomorrow at 9:30 to review your roof and solar options. We'll make sure the design accounts for your spring timeline. Looking forward to it.' That text also serves as a soft reminder to send the bill. The passive close ('look forward to hearing back from you') puts the next action entirely on Ken — the wrong direction for a conditional booking.
